lede: COSI is the research lab of Professor Stefano A. Bini, MD, at UCSF. We work on hip and knee surgery using machine learning, large clinical datasets, biomechanical modeling, and data from surgical robots. The aim is to change how these operations are planned and carried out.
bone_caption: Derived from a machine-learning segmentation of an MRI hip scan.
manifesto1: Surgical robots log every cut and every gap. Record networks hold the histories of tens of millions of patients. A tendon repair can be simulated before the incision is made.
manifesto2: The raw material for a new kind of surgery *already exists*. Our job is to turn it into evidence and tools surgeons can use.
research_intro: Five programs that feed each other. Clinical data raises mechanical questions; the models we build to answer them point to new techniques; those techniques, used in the operating room, produce the next round of data.
prog1_desc: Screening entire classes of drugs and exposures against musculoskeletal disease in the records of tens of millions of patients.
prog2_desc: Patient-specific simulations of surgical repairs: how a construct shares load, and where it fails, before the operating room.
prog3_desc: Treating the robot's plan as a prediction, and testing it against what the surgeon actually finds.
prog4_title: AI agents in the clinical workflow
prog4_desc: Agentic software for evidence synthesis, cohort building, and documentation, held to a clinical standard.
prog5_desc: New approaches and repair strategies, taken from anatomic insight to the operating room, then measured.
work1_title: A medication-wide association study of hip and knee osteoarthritis across 50 drugs
work1_desc: Screening dozens of medications at once for their association with osteoarthritis in a federated clinical dataset.
work2_title: Load-sharing mechanics of scaffold augmentation in gluteus medius repair
work2_desc: How a scaffold redistributes load across a tendon repair, modeled patient by patient.
work3_title: Native coronal deformity and gap prediction in robotic-assisted knee replacement
work3_desc: Why the robot underpredicts the medial gap as native deformity increases.
involve1_desc: Take on research with real clinical impact, publish it, and work alongside talented engineers and clinicians.
involve2_desc: Pilot technology with the surgeons who will use it and a lab that will measure whether it works.

news.text: DOCSF, the Digital Orthopaedics Conference founded by our principal investigator, returns to San Francisco.
Our first peer-reviewed paper, a systematic review of biologic augmentation in gluteus medius tendon repair, appears in Orthopaedic Reviews.
We are taking on students, residents, and collaborators on a rolling basis.
